context: Current closing price of ResMed (RMD): $219.85 USD as of 2026-04-24.
justification_text:
My point estimates reflect a modest upward drift from what I assessed as the prevailing price level at the time of the forecast. For the one-day horizon, I anchored closely to the last observed price, adding only a small expected drift given that intraday momentum and short-term mean reversion tendencies tend to dominate over very short windows. For the one-week estimate, I projected slightly higher, reflecting a gentle positive drift assumption consistent with broad equity market trends and the absence of any immediately known catalysts that would dramatically reprice the asset. The one-month estimate continued this gradual upward trajectory, incorporating a longer compounding of that drift while remaining conservative enough not to extrapolate aggressively beyond recent trading ranges.

The confidence interval widths were calibrated primarily to historical realized volatility at each corresponding time horizon. For one day, volatility is relatively contained, so the interval is tight — roughly plus or minus four percent around the point estimate. As the horizon extends to one week and one month, uncertainty compounds in proportion to the square root of time, which is why the monthly interval is substantially wider, spanning over thirty points. I also factored in that longer horizons introduce more structural uncertainty: macroeconomic data releases, Federal Reserve communications, earnings announcements, and geopolitical developments all accumulate in ways that are impossible to forecast precisely.

Specific risks that could push outcomes outside the stated intervals include a sharp deterioration in macroeconomic sentiment, such as an unexpected inflation print or a surprise shift in central bank policy, which could send prices meaningfully below the lower bound of even the monthly interval. On the upside, a strong risk-on rally driven by better-than-expected economic data or a resolution of geopolitical tensions could push prices beyond the upper bound. Liquidity events or correlated selling across asset classes — such as those triggered by credit stress or a sudden de-risking by institutional investors — represent tail risks that my intervals do not fully capture, as they are by design meant to cover only ninety percent of the probability mass, leaving ten percent allocated to these more extreme scenarios.
